topics.topicTitle: Knee Pain Differential: Structural vs Inflammatory vs Referred

lessons.lessonTitle: Diagnosing and Managing Knee Pain in the Outpatient NP Setting
objectives: Distinguish structural, inflammatory, and referred causes of knee pain using history and targeted physical exam
Apply the Ottawa Knee Rules to determine imaging necessity
Identify the red flags that mandate joint aspiration or emergency referral
Select appropriate first-line management for OA, gout, and patellofemoral pain

keyPoints: Osteoarthritis: age >45, insidious onset, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, bony enlargement, no systemic features; X-ray confirms joint space narrowing in atypical cases
Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS): anterior knee pain worsened by stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (movie sign); Clarke's test positive; no effusion; young active patients
Meniscal injury: medial or lateral joint line tenderness, McMurray's test positive, effusion common; acute tears may lock; MRI required before surgical decision
Ligament sprains: mechanism-driven (valgus stress MCL, pivot-deceleration ACL); grade I-II MCL conservative; ACL complete tear requires orthopedic referral
Prepatellar bursitis: discrete anterior patellar swelling; occupation-related (kneeling); aspirate only if septic bursitis suspected
Gout: acute monoarthritis peaking in 24h, erythema, extreme tenderness; negatively birefringent needle-shaped urate crystals under polarized light; serum uric acid may be normal during acute attack
Pseudogout (CPPD): clinically mimics gout; positively birefringent rhomboid crystals; chondrocalcinosis on X-ray; common in elderly; same acute treatment as gout

Septic arthritis (EMERGENCY): fever + acute hot swollen joint; any age; risk factors include immunosuppression, recent injection, skin breach; WBC >50,000 in aspirate; joint aspiration is mandatory before empiric antibiotics
Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric polyarthritis, MCP/PIP/wrist predominance, stiffness >1h; RF and anti-CCP positive; knee involvement in established disease; refer to rheumatology
Hip OA commonly refers pain to anterior knee or medial thigh; knee exam is normal; restricted hip internal rotation on exam is the key clue
L3-L4 lumbar radiculopathy may produce anterior thigh and knee pain; positive femoral nerve stretch test; associated low back pain
Clinical rule: always examine the hip in every knee complaint, especially when knee findings do not match the symptom severity

X-ray indicated if any one criterion present: age ≥55, isolated patellar tenderness, fibular head tenderness, inability to flex to 90 degrees, inability to bear weight (4 steps) at time of injury AND in clinic
MRI indicated for suspected ligament or meniscal injury in a surgical candidate, or when X-ray is negative but significant functional loss persists
OA is a clinical diagnosis in most cases; imaging confirms but does not replace clinical judgment

OA first-line: exercise therapy, weight loss, acetaminophen PRN; topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) preferred over oral NSAIDs in older adults with renal risk; intra-articular corticosteroid injections for moderate flares
Gout acute: colchicine 0.6mg BID, or NSAIDs, or prednisone if contraindicated; long-term ULT (allopurinol) if ≥2 flares/year or tophi; target uric acid <6 mg/dL
Septic arthritis: do not treat empirically without aspiration confirmation; send to ED for joint washout and IV antibiotics
Escalation triggers: failure of 6-8 weeks conservative therapy, suspected surgical lesion, septic arthritis, new mechanical locking

lessons.teachingNotes: The NP's highest-stakes error in knee pain is attributing fever and monoarthritis to gout without excluding septic arthritis. These can coexist. Any acute hot joint with fever requires aspiration. For OA: resist escalating to NSAIDs before recommending PT and weight loss — these have the best long-term outcomes. For referred pain: always examine the hip; a completely normal knee exam with anterior thigh pain points away from the knee.

topics.topicTitle: UTI Management: Uncomplicated vs Complicated, Antibiotic Stewardship

lessons.lessonTitle: Evidence-Based UTI Management with Stewardship Principles
objectives: Classify UTI as uncomplicated or complicated using clinical criteria
Select first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis using stewardship principles
Apply pregnancy-specific treatment and screening protocols
Avoid overtreating asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly and non-pregnant adults

keyPoints: Uncomplicated: dysuria, frequency, urgency in a non-pregnant healthy adult woman with no structural abnormalities; clinical diagnosis acceptable without culture

Complicated: any of — male sex, pregnancy, diabetes, indwelling catheter, structural abnormality, immunocompromise, symptoms >7 days, prior treatment failure, recent urinary instrumentation; culture mandatory
Pyelonephritis features: fever ≥38°C, costovertebral angle tenderness, nausea/vomiting, systemic illness; WBC casts on urinalysis are pathognomonic for upper tract involvement

Uncomplicated cystitis first-line: nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100mg BID x5 days — preferred for low resistance, minimal collateral damage; AVOID if CrCl <30 or pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration)
TMP-SMX DS BID x3 days — use only when local resistance <20%; avoid first trimester and at term
Fosfomycin 3g sachet single dose — convenient; effective against E. coli; less effective for Klebsiella
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin): NEVER first-line for uncomplicated cystitis; reserve for complicated UTI and pyelonephritis; high collateral damage, tendinopathy risk
Pyelonephritis oral outpatient: ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x7 days or TMP-SMX DS BID x14 days (culture-guided); admit if unable to tolerate orals, pregnant, or severely ill
Screen all pregnant patients for ASB at first prenatal visit; treat all positive cultures regardless of symptoms — reduces pyelonephritis and preterm labor
Safe antibiotics in pregnancy: nitrofurantoin (2nd trimester preferred; avoid ≥36 weeks), cephalexin (all trimesters), amoxicillin-clavulanate
Avoid in pregnancy: TMP-SMX (1st trimester folate antagonism; late term kernicterus risk), fluoroquinolones (theoretical cartilage risk)
Pyelonephritis in pregnancy: hospitalize in most cases; risk of preterm labor and sepsis

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Asymptomatic Bacteriuria — When Not to Treat
keyPoints: ASB is extremely common in elderly (>50% of institutionalized women) and catheterized patients; treatment increases resistance without benefit
Treat ASB ONLY in: pregnancy, and pre-urological procedure patients
Bacteriuria plus non-specific symptoms (confusion, falls) in elderly does not equal UTI — evaluate for other causes before prescribing antibiotics
Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI): treat only if symptomatic; remove or replace catheter if possible; culture-directed 5-7 days
Definition: ≥2 UTIs in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months; send urine culture each episode
Postmenopausal women: topical vaginal estrogen cream is the most effective evidence-based non-antibiotic prevention strategy
Post-coital prophylaxis: single dose nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX after intercourse if coitally related
Continuous low-dose prophylaxis: nitrofurantoin 50-100mg nightly x6-12 months for frequent recurrence

lessons.teachingNotes: The two most important stewardship mistakes in UTI: (1) prescribing fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis — this is a top antibiotic stewardship violation; (2) treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in the elderly without symptoms. In pregnancy, the calculus reverses — ASB always requires treatment. For the NP, developing a reflexive pattern of 'uncomplicated → nitrofurantoin, pyelonephritis → ciprofloxacin + culture first' covers the majority of presentations.

lessons.lessonTitle: Comorbidity-Driven Drug Selection and Prescribing Specifics Beyond Metformin
objectives: Identify when T2DM intensification is indicated based on A1c targets and clinical context
Select second-line agents using comorbidity-driven logic (ASCVD, HF, CKD, weight)
Apply prescribing specifics for GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors including dosing and contraindications
Individualize A1c targets in older adults and frail patients to avoid hypoglycemia harm

keyPoints: Intensify when A1c not at individualized goal after 3 months of optimized metformin
At diagnosis: dual therapy or insulin if A1c ≥10% or symptomatic hyperglycemia (polyuria, weight loss, polydipsia)
A1c targets: <7.0% for most healthy adults; 7.5-8.0% for complex older adults; <8.5% for frail with limited life expectancy — avoid hypoglycemia as primary harm in frail elderly
Mechanism: incretin mimetic — glucose-dependent insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, delayed gastric emptying, satiety; low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk
Semaglutide SC (Ozempic): start 0.25mg weekly x4 weeks → 0.5mg → 1mg → 2mg at 4-week intervals; proven MACE reduction in established ASCVD (SUSTAIN-6)
Semaglutide oral (Rybelsus): 3mg daily x1 month → 7mg → 14mg; must take on empty stomach with ≤120mL water, no food for 30 minutes after
Contraindications: personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma, MEN2 syndrome; use with caution in prior pancreatitis
Side effects: nausea/vomiting/diarrhea during titration; rare pancreatitis and gallbladder disease; nausea resolves in 4-8 weeks for most patients
Mechanism: block renal glucose reabsorption (glycosuria), reduce sodium reabsorption; BP and weight reduction are secondary benefits
Empagliflozin 10-25mg daily; dapagliflozin 10mg daily — both approved for HFrEF regardless of T2DM status
CKD protection: dapagliflozin and empagliflozin slow GFR decline; renal benefit persists to eGFR ≥20 for HF/CKD indication; glycemic efficacy diminishes <45
Hold 3 days before major surgery — euglycemic DKA risk
Euglycemic DKA: normal or mildly elevated glucose with significant ketonemia — do not dismiss because glucose is normal; stop SGLT-2 and send to ED
Contraindications: eGFR <30 for glycemic indication; Type 1 DM; recurrent genital mycotic infections (relative)
Established ASCVD: GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) or SGLT-2 inhibitor — both reduce MACE; GLP-1 RA generally preferred for weight loss benefit
HFrEF: SGLT-2 inhibitor first (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) — strongest HHF reduction evidence; AVOID saxagliptin (increased HHF, SAVOR-TIMI) and pioglitazone (fluid retention)
CKD stage 3b-4: SGLT-2 inhibitor for nephroprotection; GLP-1 RA safe; linagliptin preferred DPP-4 (no renal dose adjustment); avoid glyburide
Weight loss priority: GLP-1 RA > SGLT-2 > DPP-4; avoid sulfonylureas and insulin if possible
Hypoglycemia prevention is the primary goal in frail elderly — sulfonylureas (especially glyburide) and insulin carry high risk
Preferred agents in elderly: linagliptin (no renal dose adjustment, no hypoglycemia), GLP-1 RA (no hypoglycemia, weight neutral at lower risk)
Glyburide is Beers Criteria — avoid in all older adults; glipizide immediate release is less dangerous if sulfonylurea required
Renal dosing: hold metformin if eGFR <30; sitagliptin requires dose reduction at CrCl <50; calculate CrCl by Cockcroft-Gault in elderly (eGFR overestimates function)

lessons.teachingNotes: The paradigm shift in T2DM intensification: drug selection is now driven by comorbidity (ASCVD, HF, CKD, weight) more than glycemic potency alone. A patient with confirmed ASCVD or HFrEF has a clear evidence-based second agent. The NP must know saxagliptin + HF = contraindicated and pioglitazone + HF = contraindicated. Euglycemic DKA on SGLT-2 is the most frequently missed adverse effect — the key is: normal glucose does NOT exclude DKA in this drug class.

topics.topicTitle: COPD: GOLD Staging, Inhaler Selection, and Exacerbation Management

lessons.lessonTitle: GOLD 2024 Classification, Inhaler Escalation, and Exacerbation Management
objectives: Apply GOLD 2024 ABE classification to determine appropriate initial maintenance therapy
Select long-acting bronchodilator class based on symptom burden and exacerbation history
Use blood eosinophil count to guide ICS addition decisions
Manage moderate COPD exacerbations outpatient with appropriate antibiotic and steroid selection

keyPoints: Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator spirometry: FEV1/FVC <0.70 — do NOT diagnose on symptoms alone
GOLD spirometric grades: Grade 1 FEV1 ≥80%, Grade 2 FEV1 50-79%, Grade 3 FEV1 30-49%, Grade 4 FEV1 <30% predicted
Spirometry grades inform prognosis but do NOT drive inhaler selection decisions — ABE classification does

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: GOLD 2024 ABE Classification and Initial Inhaler Selection
keyPoints: ABE uses symptom burden (CAT ≥10 or mMRC ≥2 = more symptoms) AND exacerbation history (Group E = ≥2 moderate exacerbations OR ≥1 hospitalization)
Group A: fewer symptoms, 0-1 uncomplicated exacerbations — any bronchodilator (LAMA preferred for persistent symptoms)
Group B: more symptoms (CAT ≥10), 0-1 uncomplicated exacerbations — LAMA monotherapy; LAMA+LABA if highly symptomatic
Group E: ≥2 moderate exacerbations or ≥1 hospitalization — LAMA+LABA dual bronchodilator as initial therapy; add ICS if eosinophils ≥300 or ongoing exacerbations
LABA monotherapy is NOT preferred for COPD maintenance — LAMA has superior evidence; LABA alone is not recommended as single long-acting bronchodilator
ICS is NOT first-line for all COPD — add only for eosinophilic phenotype or frequent exacerbators
Add ICS when: blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL AND/OR Group E classification on dual bronchodilator
ICS increases pneumonia risk — avoid in patients with recurrent pneumonia, eosinophils <100, or mycobacterial infection
Triple therapy (LAMA+LABA+ICS): IMPACT trial showed mortality benefit in eosinophilic frequent exacerbators
Antibiotics indicated when Anthonisen criteria met: increased sputum volume + increased sputum purulence + increased dyspnea (all 3, or 2 of 3 in moderate-severe exacerbation)
First-line antibiotic: azithromycin 500mg day 1 then 250mg x4 days; doxycycline 100mg BID x5 days; amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125mg BID x5-7 days
Systemic steroids: prednisone 40mg daily x5 days — no benefit to longer courses (REDUCE trial); do not use inhaled ICS alone for exacerbations
Oxygen target in known/suspected CO2 retainers: SpO2 88-92% — avoid driving above 92-94% to prevent CO2 narcosis from suppressed hypoxic drive
Admission criteria: SpO2 <88% not responding to oxygen, acute respiratory failure, failure of outpatient therapy at 72h, inability to tolerate oral medications, poor social support

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Device Selection and Older Adult Considerations
keyPoints: Dry powder inhalers (DPIs) require sufficient inspiratory flow — in severe COPD or frail elderly, inadequate inspiratory effort limits delivery
Soft-mist inhalers (SMIs, e.g., Spiriva Respimat) do not require high inspiratory flow — preferred when DPI technique is inadequate
MDI with spacer improves delivery when coordination is poor; assess technique at every clinic visit
Cardioselective beta-blockers are generally safe in COPD — do not withhold for CAD or HF management without compelling reason

lessons.teachingNotes: Three common prescribing errors in COPD: (1) starting with SABA alone as sole long-term therapy — this is symptom-reactive, not maintenance; (2) adding ICS without eosinophilic indication — increases pneumonia risk; (3) continuing oral steroids beyond 5 days for exacerbation — no added benefit and significant harm. Oxygen targeting 88-92% is a critical outpatient skill for COPD patients who present in exacerbation.

topics.topicTitle: Headache Differential: Benign vs Emergent Red Flags

lessons.lessonTitle: Red Flag Identification and Safe Disposition in Headache Presentations
objectives: Apply the SNOOP4 framework to identify headache red flags requiring urgent evaluation
Recognize thunderclap headache as SAH until proven otherwise and initiate the correct workup sequence
Diagnose giant cell arteritis and initiate same-day treatment to prevent vision loss
Differentiate migraine, tension-type, and cluster headache and select appropriate management

keyPoints: S — Systemic symptoms: fever, stiff neck, rash, weight loss, immunocompromise
N — Neurological signs: focal deficit, diplopia, papilledema, altered mentation
O — Onset: thunderclap (maximal in seconds) — SAH until proven otherwise
O2 — Older age: new headache pattern after age 50 (mass, GCA, malignancy)
P — Papilledema: elevated ICP
P2 — Positional: worse supine (ICP elevation) or upright (low CSF pressure)
P3 — Progressive worsening over days to weeks
P4 — Pattern change: significant change from patient's established headache

SAH: thunderclap onset, maximal intensity in seconds to 1 minute; workup: non-contrast CT head first (90-98% sensitive <6h), then LP for xanthochromia if CT negative; CT alone does NOT exclude SAH — LP is required with high clinical suspicion

GCA (Temporal arteritis): age >50, new temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, ESR >50, elevated CRP; action: start prednisone 40-60mg/day IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for biopsy; biopsy remains valid for 2 weeks after steroid initiation; vision loss from AION is irreversible

Bacterial meningitis: fever + headache + neck stiffness + photophobia; petechial rash = meningococcemia requiring immediate antibiotics; CT first only if papilledema or focal deficit present; do not delay antibiotics for imaging in immunocompetent patient with classic triad
PComm aneurysm: fixed dilated non-reactive ipsilateral pupil (mydriasis, not miosis) + headache = CN III compression; CT angiography urgently; do NOT treat as migraine
CO poisoning: headache in enclosed space, multiple people affected, improves with fresh air; standard SpO2 useless (CO-oximetry required)
Migraine: POUND mnemonic — Pulsating, 4-72 hOurs, Unilateral, Nausea, Disabling; photophobia/phonophobia; aura in 30%; classic presentation in reproductive-age women
Tension-type: bilateral band-like, mild-moderate, no nausea, not worsened by activity; most common headache type overall; acetaminophen or NSAIDs first-line

Cluster: periorbital unilateral, 15-180 min, 1-8/day, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, Horner's), restlessness (pathognomonic); predominantly male; high-flow O2 + injectable sumatriptan are first-line acute treatments
Medication overuse headache (MOH): analgesic/triptan use ≥10-15 days/month for >3 months; daily or near-daily headache pattern; treatment is analgesic withdrawal + preventive therapy
Acute mild-moderate: NSAIDs or acetaminophen; acute moderate-severe: triptans (sumatriptan 50-100mg PO or 6mg SC) — fastest onset SC; gepants (rimegepant) safe in cardiovascular disease
Prophylaxis indications: ≥4 migraines/month, migraines lasting >48h, or significant disability (MIDAS ≥11)
Prophylaxis options: topiramate 25-100mg/day, propranolol 40-240mg/day, amitriptyline 25-75mg nightly, CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab)
Avoid in women of reproductive age: valproate (Category X teratogen) and topiramate (also teratogenic — REMS program, contraceptive counseling required)

lessons.teachingNotes: The NP's primary obligation in headache is not to diagnose the headache type but to reliably identify who cannot be safely managed outpatient. First thunderclap headache = SAH protocol regardless of what the patient calls it. GCA requires same-day steroids before biopsy — vision loss is the permanent consequence of delay. Cluster headache is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine or trigeminal neuralgia; the restlessness (cannot sit still) differentiates it from migraine (patients want to lie in a dark quiet room).

topics.topicTitle: Syncope Differential: Vasovagal vs Cardiac vs Neurological

lessons.lessonTitle: Risk Stratification and Disposition in Syncope
objectives: Identify clinical features that distinguish vasovagal from cardiac syncope
Apply validated risk stratification tools to determine safe outpatient vs ED disposition
Recognize ECG patterns that mandate emergency referral
Manage vasovagal syncope conservatively with education and counterpressure techniques

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Vasovagal vs Cardiac Syncope — Discriminating Features
keyPoints: Vasovagal: prodrome required (nausea, diaphoresis, blurred vision lasting seconds to minutes); positional trigger (prolonged standing, hot environment); situational (pain, emotion, blood draw); brief LOC <60 seconds; rapid complete recovery
Cardiac arrhythmic: no prodrome or brief (<5 sec); occurs seated or supine; palpitations preceding LOC; prolonged recovery; exertional syncope; family history of sudden cardiac death

Postictal confusion >5-10 minutes after regaining consciousness is the most discriminating feature for seizure over syncope; brief myoclonic jerks during syncope (anoxic myoclonus, <10 sec) do NOT indicate epilepsy

Orthostatic hypotension: defined as BP drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes of standing; common causes — dehydration, medications (antihypertensives, alpha-blockers, diuretics, TCAs), autonomic neuropathy

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: ECG Red Flags Requiring ED Referral
keyPoints: QTc prolongation >470ms (female) or >450ms (male) — risk of torsades de pointes and polymorphic VT
Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation in V1-V2 with RBBB morphology
Delta wave (short PR + slurred QRS upstroke) — WPW; high risk if AF develops
Epsilon wave in V1-V3 — ARVC (arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy)
Complete heart block or pauses >3 seconds on monitoring
New LBBB in the context of syncope — consider structural heart disease
Exertional syncope = structural cardiac disease until proven otherwise: HOCM, severe aortic stenosis, pulmonary arterial hypertension
HOCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the US; outflow obstruction worsens with exertion
Mandatory workup for exertional syncope: ECG, echocardiogram, same-day or next-day cardiology; no return to sports without clearance
Family history of sudden cardiac death + young patient + exertional syncope = emergent evaluation
High-risk features mandating ED or hospital: exertional syncope, abnormal ECG, structural heart disease, no prodrome in older adult, troponin elevation, HF signs, oxygen saturation <94%
Low-risk (outpatient safe): age <40, classic vasovagal story, normal ECG, no structural disease, normal vital signs, symmetric prodrome
San Francisco Syncope Rule high-risk: HF history, hematocrit <30%, abnormal ECG, shortness of breath, SBP <90 at presentation
ECG is mandatory for ALL patients presenting with syncope — this is the single most important initial test
Education and reassurance: benign prognosis when thoroughly evaluated and no cardiac etiology
Counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing, arm tensing, squatting when prodrome begins) — proven to abort or delay many episodes
Increase fluid and salt intake if not contraindicated; avoid prolonged standing, hot environments, dehydration
Midodrine 5-10mg TID (alpha-agonist) for recurrent orthostatic-vasovagal; fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily for volume-depleted phenotype
Driving restriction: document discussion; most states require reporting to DMV for recurrent or high-risk syncope

lessons.teachingNotes: The urgent care NP's primary role in syncope is not to diagnose the exact mechanism but to triage: who goes to the ED now, who sees cardiology tomorrow, and who can be safely reassured. A normal ECG and classic vasovagal story in a young person is genuinely low-risk. The same presentation in an older adult or anyone with structural disease is not. The mandatory ECG is non-negotiable.

topics.topicTitle: Red Flags in Hypertension: When to Refer or Admit

lessons.lessonTitle: Distinguishing Hypertensive Emergency from Urgency and Directing Appropriate Care
objectives: Define hypertensive emergency versus urgency using end-organ damage criteria
Identify the most dangerous individual presentations within hypertensive emergency
Avoid common errors including sublingual nifedipine use and premature aggressive lowering in urgency
Recognize resistant hypertension and initiate secondary workup

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Emergency vs Urgency — The Defining Distinction
keyPoints: Hypertensive urgency: BP ≥180/120 WITHOUT acute end-organ damage; patient may have headache or anxiety; managed with oral agents, rest, and 24-48h follow-up
Hypertensive emergency: BP ≥180/120 WITH acute end-organ damage — brain, eye, heart, kidney, aorta, placenta; requires ED transfer for IV management

End-organ damage markers: new confusion/altered mentation, papilledema, flash pulmonary edema, ACS (chest pain + ECG changes), aortic dissection (tearing pain + inter-arm differential), AKI (creatinine doubling), eclampsia

Aortic dissection: tearing ripping chest or back pain + inter-arm systolic BP difference >20 mmHg; call 911; target SBP <120 with IV beta-blocker FIRST (esmolol or labetalol) to reduce HR AND BP, then vasodilator — NEVER vasodilator alone (reflex tachycardia increases aortic wall shear force)
Hypertensive encephalopathy: confusion + severe headache + elevated BP; PRES on MRI; reduce MAP 10-15% in first hour, then 15% over next 23 hours — NOT rapid normalization

Eclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + seizure or severe features in pregnant/postpartum patient; magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis; labetalol or hydralazine IV; call obstetrics and 911; delivery is definitive treatment
HELLP syndrome: elevated BP + elevated LFTs + hemolysis + thrombocytopenia + RUQ pain in pregnancy/postpartum — obstetric emergency

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Urgency Management — What NOT to Do
keyPoints: Do NOT administer sublingual nifedipine — this causes rapid uncontrolled BP drop precipitating stroke and MI; it is dangerous and specifically contraindicated
Do NOT rapidly lower BP in urgency — rapid lowering in chronically hypertensive patients causes ischemia of brain and heart (autoregulatory range reset)
Correct approach: have patient rest 20-30 minutes in quiet room, restart or adjust oral antihypertensives, arrange 24-48h follow-up
Urgency does NOT require hospital admission — close outpatient follow-up is appropriate when no end-organ damage is present
Definition: BP above goal despite ≥3 antihypertensive agents at maximum tolerated doses including a diuretic, with confirmed adherence
Add spironolactone 25-50mg as 4th agent — most effective add-on; primary hyperaldosteronism is highly prevalent in resistant HTN

Secondary cause workup: serum aldosterone/plasma renin ratio, TSH, sleep study (OSA very common), renal artery duplex (bruit + flash pulmonary edema + ACEi-induced AKI), 24h urinary catecholamines/metanephrines if pheochromocytoma suspected
Isolated systolic HTN (SBP ≥140, DBP <90) most common pattern in elderly — target SBP 130-140 in non-frail; 140-150 acceptable in frail with orthostasis
Check standing BP before intensifying therapy — orthostatic hypotension from antihypertensives increases fall risk more than the BP reduction helps
NSAIDs raise BP substantially in elderly patients on antihypertensives — review OTC NSAID use at every visit
Amlodipine-induced dependent edema in older adults is NOT heart failure — reduce dose or switch class before adding diuretics

lessons.teachingNotes: The sublingual nifedipine prohibition is one of the most important clinical pearls in hypertension management — it is still occasionally used despite being dangerous. The key clinical skill is distinguishing urgency (BP elevated, patient uncomfortable, no organ damage) from emergency (BP elevated + organ under acute attack). The exam and history should be systematic: check neuro, check vision, check chest, check renal function.

topics.topicTitle: Cancer Red Flags in Primary Care: When to Expedite Workup

lessons.lessonTitle: Recognizing Alarm Features and Expediting Cancer Detection in Primary Care
objectives: Identify ALARM features for GI, urological, pulmonary, and breast malignancy
Apply urgency tiers to determine 2-week vs routine referral
Recognize constitutional red flags (weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy) that suggest systemic malignancy
Apply USPSTF screening thresholds for lung cancer in high-risk adults

keyPoints: Unexplained weight loss >5% body weight over 6-12 months without clear cause warrants systematic cancer workup: CBC, CMP, LDH, LFTs, UA, TSH, CXR; age >50 add PSA and colonoscopy
B-symptoms (drenching night sweats + weight loss + ± fever) + lymphadenopathy = lymphoma pattern — urgent CBC with differential, LDH, uric acid, CT scan
Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node on left) is ALWAYS a red flag — represents drainage from thorax and abdomen; never observe without workup; urgent CT and hematology/oncology
ALARM criteria: Anemia (iron deficiency — bidirectional GI endoscopy until source found), Loss of weight, Anorexia, Recent onset symptoms in older patient, Melaena/hematemesis
Progressive dysphagia (solids then liquids) = obstructive lesion advancing — urgent EGD within 2 weeks regardless of GERD history
Rectal bleeding in patient >40 without clearly benign anorectal source — colorectal cancer workup; do NOT assume hemorrhoids without adequate evaluation
Change in bowel habits >6 weeks in adult >50 — colonoscopy

Gross (visible) hematuria in adult ≥35: CT urogram + cystoscopy referral to urology — mandatory regardless of whether current UA is negative (bleeding is intermittent); bladder cancer and RCC present this way
Hemoptysis in smoker >40: CT chest + pulmonology referral; bronchoscopy if CT negative but clinical suspicion remains

Lung cancer screening: LDCT annually for adults age 50-80 with ≥20 pack-year history AND current smoker or quit within the past 15 years (USPSTF 2021); chest X-ray does NOT have screening mortality benefit
Palpable breast mass in any age: diagnostic mammogram + breast ultrasound; do NOT rely on prior screening mammogram; new palpable mass needs evaluation regardless of recent imaging
Postmenopausal breast mass with firm, non-mobile character: urgent imaging + surgical/breast specialist referral within 1-2 weeks

Skin: ABCDE rule — Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution (any change) = urgent dermatology referral or biopsy; non-healing ulcerated nodule in sun-exposed area = SCC risk

lessons.teachingNotes: The critical discipline in cancer red flags is urgency tiers: visible hematuria and supraclavicular adenopathy are 1-2 week referrals, not next available appointments. A prior normal mammogram does not reassure a new palpable mass — explain this clearly in patient education. For lung screening, the 15-year quit cutoff is commonly missed — patients who quit smoking 14 years ago still qualify.

topics.topicTitle: PrEP for HIV Prevention: Eligibility, Prescribing, and Monitoring

lessons.lessonTitle: Inclusive PrEP Prescribing: Eligibility, Regimen Selection, and LGBTQ+ Clinical Adaptations
objectives: Determine PrEP eligibility using behavioral and clinical criteria without assumptions about identity
Select the correct PrEP regimen based on exposure anatomy and renal function — including Descovy's vaginal exposure restriction
Apply the mandatory monitoring schedule for all PrEP regimens
Conduct an inclusive, anatomy-based sexual history using language that does not presuppose gender or orientation

keyPoints: Substantial risk criteria: MSM with condomless anal sex and unknown-status partners, recent bacterial STI, serodiscordant couple (partner HIV+), PWID with shared equipment, heterosexual with high-prevalence partner population
MANDATORY before prescribing: confirmed HIV-negative status within 7 days of initiation — a 6-month-old negative test is NOT adequate (patient may have acquired HIV since then)
Use 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test or HIV RNA for pre-initiation — highest sensitivity for acute infection
Baseline labs: HIV test, hepatitis B antigen and antibody, hepatitis C antibody, creatinine/eGFR, STI screening (site-specific NAAT — urethral/rectal/pharyngeal), pregnancy test if applicable

TDF/FTC (Truvada): approved for all eligible groups including cisgender women for vaginal exposure; generic available; monitor renal function every 6 months (TDF can reduce tubular function and bone density)

TAF/FTC (Descovy): approved for MSM, transgender women, and PWID — NOT approved for receptive vaginal sex (not studied in cisgender women or transgender men for vaginal exposure); better renal and bone safety profile; preferred if CrCl 30-59 or bone density concern

Cabotegravir injectable (Apretude): 600mg IM, first two doses 1 month apart, then every 2 months; no daily pill required; superior efficacy shown in HPTN 083 (MSM/TGW) and HPTN 084 (cisgender women); addresses pill fatigue, privacy concerns
Protection timeline: TDF/FTC protects rectal tissue after 7 days of consistent use; vaginal tissue and injection drug use require 21 days — this difference reflects tissue concentration kinetics
HIV test every 3 months — this is non-negotiable; if reactive at any visit: STOP PrEP immediately, send HIV RNA, urgent HIV specialist referral within days for resistance testing before starting ART
STI screening every 3-6 months: rectal, urethral, AND pharyngeal site-specific NAAT — do NOT rely on genital swab alone; site-specific testing triples STI detection in MSM
Renal function (CrCl): every 6 months for Truvada; annually for Descovy; Descovy approved to eGFR ≥30
Hepatitis C annually for MSM and PWID; HBV baseline and vaccination if not immune

Inclusive history: use anatomical and behavioral language — ask about anatomy, types of sex acts, and partner anatomy rather than identity labels; a patient identifying as straight may have same-sex partners
Anal Pap cytology: strongly recommended for HIV-positive MSM; offer and discuss for HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners (high HPV burden, elevated anal HSIL risk)
Transgender women on estrogen: no significant drug interactions with TDF/FTC or TAF/FTC; both Truvada and Descovy approved; injectable cabotegravir addresses pill fatigue common in this population
Transgender men on testosterone: Truvada is the appropriate choice for vaginal exposure (Descovy not approved); assess relationship to risk based on anatomy and sexual practices

Mental health and substance use screening at every visit — LGBTQ+ patients have 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, and substance use; methamphetamine and poppers (amyl nitrite) use in chemsex contexts
If HIV test becomes reactive while on PrEP: stop PrEP immediately — continuing TDF/FTC with active HIV selects for resistance to these drugs (M184V, K65R mutations)
Obtain resistance testing on the reactive sample before starting ART — drug selection must be informed by resistance profile
Do NOT add a single agent (dolutegravir, etc.) to TDF/FTC without full resistance workup — inadequate regimen worsens resistance
Urgent HIV specialist referral within days — document timeline and actions taken

lessons.teachingNotes: The most clinically critical fact in PrEP prescribing: Descovy is NOT approved for vaginal exposure in cisgender women or transgender men — this is a regulatory and clinical distinction that directly affects prescribing decisions. The 7-day vs 21-day protection window is the second most tested prescribing nuance. HIV testing recency before initiation (within 7 days) is the patient safety cornerstone. Creating a genuinely inclusive clinical space is not just ethics — it is what enables patients to disclose information needed to prescribe correctly.

topics.topicTitle: Geriatric Assessment: Falls, Polypharmacy, Functional Status, and Frailty

lessons.lessonTitle: Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment: Falls, Frailty, and Prescribing Safety in Older Adults
objectives: Apply the STEADI falls assessment framework and Timed Up and Go test in clinical practice
Score frailty using the Fried Criteria and identify the pre-frail intervention window
Identify high-risk fall-contributing medications and deprescribe appropriately
Use Cockcroft-Gault CrCl (not eGFR) for drug dosing in elderly patients

keyPoints: Ask every patient >65 at every visit: 'Have you fallen in the past year?' and 'Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?' — falls are underreported without direct inquiry
Timed Up and Go (TUG) test: patient rises from chair, walks 10 feet, returns, sits — ≥12 seconds indicates increased fall risk; normal <10 seconds

Fall-contributing medication classes (Beers Criteria): benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (zolpidem), opioids, TCAs, first-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine), antihypertensives causing orthostasis, gabapentinoids, antipsychotics
Extrinsic risk: poor lighting, loose rugs, cluttered stairs, no grab bars in bathroom, inappropriate footwear — document home safety assessment

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Frailty — Fried Criteria and Clinical Implications
keyPoints: Five components: (1) unintentional weight loss >4.5kg/year, (2) self-reported exhaustion (CES-D), (3) weakness (grip strength below threshold), (4) slow gait speed (<0.8 m/s), (5) low physical activity
Scoring: 0 = robust; 1-2 = pre-frail (intervention window); ≥3 = frail
Pre-frailty is actionable — resistance exercise (most evidence-based intervention), protein optimization, and targeted deficit management can prevent or reverse pre-frailty progression
Frailty predicts surgical outcomes, chemotherapy tolerance, ICU mortality, and length of stay better than age alone — document frailty status when referring for procedures

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Renal Dosing in Elderly — CrCl vs eGFR
keyPoints: Drug dosing in elderly requires Cockcroft-Gault estimated CrCl, NOT CKD-EPI or MDRD eGFR — eGFR is normalized to BSA (1.73 m²) for CKD staging and overestimates function in low-muscle-mass elderly
Cockcroft-Gault formula: CrCl = [(140-age) × weight(kg)] / (72 × SCr) × 0.85 for females
Clinical example: 80-year-old woman, SCr 1.1, weight 55kg → CrCl ≈ 33 mL/min; lab eGFR may report 58 — dosing on eGFR would result in drug overdose
Dose-adjust at every medication change: sitagliptin (50mg at CrCl 30-50), gabapentin, metformin (hold <30), apixaban (calculate before prescribing)

ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, continence, feeding) — impairment indicates need for supportive services; new ADL decline is a red flag for acute illness, medication toxicity, or delirium
IADLs (phone, shopping, cooking, housekeeping, laundry, transportation, medications, finances) — more sensitive to early cognitive decline; assess at annual geriatric visit
Cognitive screening: Mini-Cog (3-word recall + clock draw) for quick screen; MoCA for more sensitive detection of MCI (10 min); MMSE less sensitive than MoCA for early cognitive impairment
Depression screening: GDS-15 (Geriatric Depression Scale) validated specifically in older adults — score ≥6 suggests probable depression

Cascade prescribing: new symptom treated as new disease without recognizing it as a medication side effect — result is layered iatrogenic harm; classic example: NSAID → ankle edema → diuretic → electrolyte problem → potassium supplement
Review ALL medications including OTCs and supplements at every visit — patients frequently take diphenhydramine (Benadryl PM), herbal supplements, or OTC NSAIDs without disclosing

Anticholinergic burden scoring (Anticholinergic Risk Scale): cumulative anticholinergic load from multiple medications increases cognitive impairment and fall risk; each unit increase in score associated with 25% fall risk increase

lessons.teachingNotes: The most impactful clinical change an NP can make for older adult patients is to ask about falls at every single visit and act on the answer. The Cockcroft-Gault vs eGFR distinction for dosing is frequently tested and clinically critical — the difference can mean the difference between therapeutic and toxic drug levels. For frailty, document your assessment — it protects patients from over-treatment and informs all specialists.

topics.topicTitle: Deprescribing in Older Adults: Beers Criteria and High-Risk Medications

lessons.lessonTitle: Identifying and Safely Stopping High-Risk Medications in Older Adults
objectives: Identify the highest-priority Beers Criteria medications and their specific harms in older adults
Apply the deprescribing process: identify, prioritize, taper vs stop, monitor
Distinguish medications requiring tapering from those that can be stopped abruptly
Recognize cascade prescribing and break the cycle at the source

keyPoints: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam, alprazolam): 2x fall risk, cognitive impairment, rebound insomnia, paradoxical agitation in dementia; alternative: CBT-I first-line; low-dose doxepin 3-6mg (FDA-approved for sleep maintenance)
Z-drugs (zolpidem, eszopiclone): same risk profile as benzodiazepines including next-day driving impairment; Beers Criteria to avoid in all older adults
First-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine, chlorpheniramine): potent anticholinergics causing confusion, urinary retention, constipation; alternative: cetirizine or loratadine for allergies
NSAIDs (regular use): 2-3x GI bleed risk, AKI risk, HTN exacerbation, CV events; alternative: acetaminophen + topical diclofenac gel

Antipsychotics in dementia: FDA black box warning — increased mortality, stroke, and pneumonia in elderly with dementia-related psychosis; non-pharmacological interventions mandatory before medication; if necessary, use lowest dose with documented informed consent
Glyburide (long-acting sulfonylurea): prolonged hypoglycemia risk in elderly who cannot perceive warning symptoms; use glipizide IR if sulfonylurea needed
Many medications carry anticholinergic properties; cumulative burden from multiple drugs compounds cognitive, GI, urinary, and fall risk

High-burden drugs: oxybutynin (bladder anticholinergic — high CNS penetration; switch to mirabegron, a beta-3 agonist with no anticholinergic effects), promethazine, hydroxyzine, tricyclic antidepressants
Anticholinergic Risk Scale or Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden Scale: score each medication; total >3 = high burden; review at every visit
Step 1: Identify all potentially inappropriate medications using Beers/STOPP criteria
Step 2: Confirm current indication — ask the patient; many prescriptions outlive their original indication
Step 3: Assess risk of stopping vs continuing for each drug
Step 4: Prioritize by highest harm (benzos and antipsychotics first)
Step 5: Shared decision-making with patient and caregiver — frame deprescribing as 'simplifying to reduce side effects,' not 'taking away medicine'

REQUIRE tapering: benzodiazepines (10% dose reduction every 2-4 weeks — abrupt stop after long-term use risks seizures), opioids (10-25% every 2-4 weeks), SSRIs/SNRIs (especially paroxetine and venlafaxine — discontinuation syndrome), beta-blockers (rebound tachycardia and angina), corticosteroids (adrenal suppression if >3 weeks use)
CAN stop abruptly: PPIs (without ongoing indication), antihistamines, statins in limited life expectancy when quality of life is the goal

Statin deprescribing in primary prevention with limited life expectancy: the NNT for cardiovascular benefit will not be realized in a patient with <12-24 month life expectancy; STOPP criteria support discontinuation with goals-of-care conversation

lessons.teachingNotes: The cascade prescribing pattern is the most preventable form of polypharmacy. Before adding a new medication, always ask: 'Could this new symptom be caused by a current medication?' Oxybutynin is the top-priority bladder anticholinergic to switch — mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) is the preferred replacement with no anticholinergic burden. Benzodiazepine tapering requires patience — 10% reductions every 2-4 weeks feels slow but prevents dangerous withdrawal in long-term users.

topics.topicTitle: Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis: Stepwise Treatment and Biologic Referral Criteria

lessons.lessonTitle: Managing Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis in Primary Care: Stepwise Therapy and Referral Timing
objectives: Distinguish psoriasis from atopic dermatitis clinically and identify features requiring urgent assessment
Apply stepwise topical therapy with appropriate potency selection by body site
Recognize PASI/DLQI thresholds and psoriatic arthritis features that trigger biologic referral
Identify eczema herpeticum as a dermatologic emergency requiring systemic antiviral therapy

conceptBlocks.blockTitle: Psoriasis — Clinical Features and Stepwise Management
keyPoints: Classic presentation: well-demarcated salmon-pink plaques with thick silvery scale; Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale removed); Koebner phenomenon; extensor surfaces, scalp, nails, lumbosacral
Psoriatic arthritis in 30%: dactylitis (sausage digit), enthesitis, asymmetric oligoarthritis — always ask about joint symptoms; PsA requires rheumatology regardless of PASI score

Stepwise treatment: mild — high-potency topical CS + calcipotriol (vitamin D analog) for maintenance (steroid-sparing, no atrophy); moderate-severe — MTX (monitor CBC, LFTs, folic acid 1mg/day), cyclosporine as bridge; biologic referral when PASI >10 or DLQI >10 with topical failure
Methotrexate monitoring: folic acid 1mg/day to reduce mucositis and hepatotoxicity; CBC and LFTs monthly initially then every 2-3 months; contraindicated in pregnancy (Category X)
Classic features: intensely pruritic, eczematous rash; flexural distribution (antecubital/popliteal fossae, neck, wrists) in adults; atopic triad with asthma and allergic rhinitis
Foundation (all severity levels): daily thick emollient application within 3 minutes of bathing (soak-and-seal technique); fragrance-free detergents; lukewarm water; avoid identified triggers

Mild-moderate: moderate-potency topical CS for trunk/extremities (triamcinolone 0.1%); low-potency for face; tacrolimus ointment or pimecrolimus cream (TCIs) for face, eyelids, and sensitive areas — steroid-sparing, safe long-term

Dupilumab (Dupixent): IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor; indicated for moderate-severe AD inadequately controlled by topicals; 300mg SC every 2 weeks; ophthalmology follow-up (conjunctivitis side effect); approved ≥6 months age; biologic referral after failure of adequate topical trial
HSV superinfection of atopic dermatitis: punched-out, monomorphic crusted vesicular lesions spreading rapidly beyond usual AD distribution; fever; systemic illness
Do NOT apply topical steroids — immune suppression worsens HSV dissemination
Treatment: systemic acyclovir 400mg 5x/day or valacyclovir 1g TID; consider hospitalization for widespread disease, ocular involvement, or immunocompromise
Bleach baths (0.5 cup per full tub, 2-3x/week) reduce S. aureus colonization and AD flare frequency — safe maintenance strategy

lessons.teachingNotes: Two high-stakes errors in dermatology for the NP: (1) missing dactylitis as a sign of psoriatic arthritis — the skin disease severity does not predict joint disease; refer to rheumatology for any joint findings; (2) applying topical steroids to eczema herpeticum — if a patient with AD develops clustered punched-out lesions and fever, think HSV and start antivirals. The biologic referral threshold (PASI >10 or DLQI >10) is the key metric to communicate clearly.

topics.topicTitle: Smoking Cessation: Pharmacotherapy Options and Counseling Strategies

lessons.lessonTitle: Evidence-Based Pharmacotherapy and Brief Counseling for Smoking Cessation
objectives: Apply the 5A framework at every patient encounter regardless of readiness to quit
Select the most appropriate cessation pharmacotherapy based on comorbidities and patient profile
Counsel patients on varenicline and bupropion dosing, contraindications, and safety
Apply NRT in pregnancy as the preferred pharmacotherapy option

keyPoints: Ask about tobacco use at EVERY visit — treat as a vital sign; document tobacco status
Advise clearly and personally: 'Quitting is the most important thing you can do for your health right now'
Assess readiness to quit: patients in precontemplation respond to 5R (Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition — return to topic at every visit)
Assist: set a quit date within 1-2 weeks; prescribe pharmacotherapy + refer to Quitline (1-800-QUIT-NOW, free, all states); brief counseling doubles quit rates
Arrange: follow-up within 1 week of quit date (highest relapse risk); at 1 month; at 3 and 6 months
Mechanism: partial alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic receptor agonist — reduces withdrawal AND reduces reward from smoking; 2-3x quit rate vs placebo; superior to NRT monotherapy and bupropion
Dosing: 0.5mg daily days 1-3 → 0.5mg BID days 4-7 → 1mg BID thereafter; start 1 week before quit date; standard 12-week course; extend to 24 weeks for high relapse risk

Safety: EAGLES trial definitively cleared cardiovascular and neuropsychiatric safety concerns — appropriate for patients with depression with monitoring; most common side effect is nausea (take with food and water)
Renal dose reduction: CrCl <30 — start 0.5mg daily, max 0.5mg BID; CrCl 30-60 — start standard, max 0.5mg BID
Mechanism: dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — reduces craving and withdrawal; dual benefit for comorbid depression; prevents post-cessation weight gain (modest)
Dosing: 150mg daily days 1-3 → 150mg BID (≥8 hours apart) starting day 4; begin 1-2 weeks before quit date; 7-12 week course

Seizure risk: lowers seizure threshold in dose-dependent manner — contraindicated in seizure disorder, anorexia/bulimia, and alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal; second SR dose must be taken by 2pm (insomnia if later)
Not serotonergic — does not cause serotonin syndrome with SSRIs; QTc prolongation is not a significant concern
Forms: patch (long-acting, background control), gum/lozenge (short-acting, PRN craving control), inhaler and nasal spray (fastest onset, prescription required)
Combination NRT (patch + gum or lozenge) is 60% more effective than patch alone — the most underutilized strategy

Pregnancy: NRT is the preferred pharmacotherapy; nicotine without tobacco toxins is significantly safer than continued smoking; avoid extended-release patches overnight in 3rd trimester; varenicline and bupropion have insufficient pregnancy safety data
OTC availability: patch, gum, lozenge available without prescription; inhaler and nasal spray require prescription

lessons.teachingNotes: The 5R framework for precontemplation patients is the most underused tool — patients who are not ready to quit today often quit within 1-2 years if the NP plants a seed at every visit. Varenicline is the most effective agent and is safe in depression after the EAGLES trial; do not avoid it in patients with psychiatric history. The critical seizure risk contraindication for bupropion (eating disorders + seizure history + alcohol withdrawal) must be reflexive. In pregnancy, NRT > continued smoking regardless of nicotine delivery.
